title: MC Pricing of a European Call

source.file: content/A Practical Guide To Quantitative Finance Interviews Book by Xinfeng Zhou (chapters)/chapter-07-algorithms-numerical-methods.md

setup.narrative: Price a European call with strike K, maturity T, on a stock with risk-neutral dynamics dS = r S dt + sigma S dW.
rules: Closed form: S_T = S_0 * exp((r - sigma^2 / 2) * T + sigma * sqrt(T) * Z) where Z ~ N(0,1).
Simulate M iid Z's, compute M values of S_T.
Estimate price: c_hat = exp(-rT) * (1/M) * sum_k max(S_T^(k) - K, 0).
Standard error: SE = (sigma_payoff / sqrt(M)) -- decays as 1/sqrt(M).
setup.question: Implement MC pricing and analyze convergence.

learningObjectives: Discretize GBM with a single step for European options.
Estimate the price as a Monte Carlo average.

stages.label: Simulate S_T
stages.prompt: What's the EXACT one-step simulation of S_T under risk-neutral GBM (no need for fine time grid for European)?

options.text: S_T = S_0 * exp((r - sigma^2 / 2) * T + sigma * sqrt(T) * Z), Z ~ N(0,1).
S_T = S_0 * (1 + r * T + sigma * sqrt(T) * Z) -- Euler.

stages.explanation: GBM has the closed-form S_T = S_0 * exp((r - sigma^2/2) T + sigma sqrt(T) Z) -- exact, not a discretization. For path-dependent options, you'd need an N-step discretization.

stages.label: Commit
stages.template: Standard error decays as 1/sqrt(__[a]__). To halve the SE, multiply paths by __[b]__.  For European options, choose number of TIME steps N = __[c]__.
stages.explanation: Halving SE requires 4x paths (since SE ~ 1/sqrt(M)). For European options, only the terminal price matters, so one step suffices.

stages.label: Reflection
prompts: How would you PRICE an Asian option (payoff depends on the average price)? (Need many time steps; can't use one-step closed form.)
What's the bias for an Asian option with N time steps? (Discretization bias O(1/N) for arithmetic Asian.)
Why is MC unattractive for AMERICAN options? (Early exercise requires backward induction; MC works forward in time.)
